title: Cryogenic Time-Division-Multiplexed Voltage Control for Scalable Trapped-Ion Quantum Processors
authors: Ryutaro Ohira, Shinichi Morisaka, Yoshinori Kurimoto, Toshiaki Inada, Ippei Nakamura, Takefumi Miyoshi, Atsushi Noguchi
abstract: Trapped-ion quantum computers based on the quantum charge-coupled device architecture require on the order of ten trap electrodes per qubit, making the number of vacuum feedthroughs a bottleneck at the system scale. Time-division multiplexed (TDM)-based voltage control for trap electrodes provides a natural route to alleviate this constraint. However, previous studies have been limited to architectural proposals for static trap-potential compensation and room-temperature demonstrations of dynamic-electrode control, leaving cryogenic operation of TDM-based voltage control for static and dynamic electrodes experimentally unexplored. In this study, we develop and cryogenically validate TDM-based voltage control schemes for two distinct electrode classes. For static electrodes used in trap-potential compensation, we implement a 32-channel demultiplexed system operating at approximately 27~K, achieving an effective voltage update rate of 37.5~kHz with an output range of $\pm10~\mathrm{V}$ per channel. For dynamic electrodes used in ion operations, such as shuttling, we implement a four-channel demultiplexed system operating at approximately 14~K, achieving an effective voltage update rate of 1~MHz with a comparable output range. These results establish TDM-based voltage control as a practical approach for both electrode classes, providing a path for mitigating the vacuum feedthrough bottleneck in scalable trapped-ion quantum processors.
summary: This paper demonstrates a cryogenic voltage control system using time-division multiplexing to reduce the number of wires needed for large-scale trapped-ion quantum processors. By successfully operating at 14 K and 27 K, the authors show that both static and dynamic electrode requirements can be met with significantly fewer vacuum feedthroughs. This approach provides a scalable path for managing the massive electrode counts required by QCCD architectures.
main_problem: The scaling of trapped-ion quantum computers using the QCCD architecture is limited by a 'wiring bottleneck,' where the massive number of electrodes required leads to an unsustainable number of vacuum feedthroughs and room-temperature DAC channels.
main_result: The researchers successfully demonstrated a cryogenic Time-Division-Multiplexed (TDM) voltage control system that achieves both static and dynamic electrode control at 14 K and 27 K, significantly reducing the required physical connections.
method: A TDM-based architecture was implemented using a room-temperature FPGA-driven DAC and a cryogenic demultiplexer (DEMUX) board, utilizing a holding capacitor at each output to retain voltage between update cycles.
model_or_system: The experimental platform is a cryogenic ion-trap system (utilizing 40-K and 4-K stages) designed for the Quantum Charge-Coupled Device (QCCD) architecture, featuring both static electrodes for potential compensation and dynamic electrodes for ion shuttling.
key_observables: Effective voltage update rates, voltage drop (leakage) over time, crosstalk between channels, and output voltage ranges.
important_parameters: Update rates of 37.5 kHz (static) and 1 MHz (dynamic); voltage range of +/- 10 V; operating temperatures of 14 K and 27 K; crosstalk levels around -12 to -35 dB.
main_assumptions: The RC time constant of the output nodes must allow for DAC output settling and capacitor charging within the allocated time slot.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the TDM system schematic and architecture for electrode classes; Figure 2 details the physical cryogenic setup and hardware components; Figure 3 displays measured DC output voltages for 32 channels; Figure 4 shows the dynamic electrode setup and measured sinusoidal waveforms.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the wiring bottleneck, describes the TDM architecture and hardware implementation, presents experimental validation for both static and dynamic electrode classes at cryogenic temperatures, and discusses scaling implications and hardware limitations.
why_interesting: This work provides a practical engineering solution to the scalability limits of trapped-ion processors, which is crucial for the realization of large-scale quantum computing architectures and complex many-body simulations in ion traps.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic of a TDM-based voltage control sys- tem for cryogenic ion traps. A representative cryogenic setup is shown, where a vacuum chamber contains 40-K and 4-K stages and the ion trap is mounted on the 4-K stage. A DAC at room temperature generates a TDM waveform at an up- date rate of Nf Hz, where N represents the number of trap electrodes. The waveform is sent to a demultiplexer (denoted as DEMUX) located at the 4-K stage, which distributes the signal to the individual electrodes. A holding capacitor con- nected to each demultiplexer output stores the voltage for the corresponding electrode via charging during the selected time slot. Consequently, each electrode is updated...

FIG. 2. TDM-based voltage control system for static elec- trodes. (a) Schematic of the system architecture. (b) Cryo- genic measurement setup with the vacuum chamber removed. (c) TDM signal generation board and DEMUX board. The TDM signal generation board, shown at the top, includes an FPGA, a DAC, operational amplifiers, and relay switches. The 32-channel DEMUX board, shown at the bottom, is in- stalled on the cryogenic stage.
FIG. 3. Measured DC output voltages of the 32-channel mul- tiplexed system at approximately 27 K.

FIG. 4. TDM-based voltage control system for dynamic elec- trodes. (a) Schematic of system architecture. (b) Cryogenic measurement setup with the vacuum chamber removed. (c) TDM signal generation board and DEMUX board, connected by a flexible printed circuit (FPC) cable. (d) Measured out- put waveforms from the four-channel demultiplexed system at approximately 14 K.
